The Atris are mentioned in the Rig-Veda as the *fathers* (*pitaras*) of mankind. They are described as having been born from the *Infinite* (*Hiranyagarbha*), or from the heavens and the earth. In some passages, they seem to represent the *ancestors* of the human race, while in others, they appear as divine or semi-divine beings. 阿特里神族在《梨俱吠陀》中被稱為人類的*祖先*（*pitaras*）。他們被描述為源自*無限者*（*Hiranyagarbha*）、或源自天地。在某些章節中，他們似乎代表人類的*祖先*，而在其他段落中，他們則表現為神聖或半神聖的存在。 The name *Atri* itself is of considerable importance in the mythology and philosophy of the Hindus. According to the Puranas, Atri was one of the seven great *Rishis* (*Saptarishis*), and is said to have been created directly by Brahman. He is represented as a great sage and lawgiver, and his descendants are supposed to have spread throughout India. *阿特里*這個名字本身在印度教的神話與哲學中具有相當重要的意義。根據《往世書》，阿特里是七大*聖仙*（*Saptarishis*）之一，據說是由梵天直接創造的。他被描繪為偉大的聖人與立法者，其後代據信遍布印度各地。 It is worthy of note that the Atris, like many other divine beings in the Rig-Veda, are connected with the phenomena of nature. They are associated with the *Maruts*, the storm-gods, and with the *Adityas*, the solar deities. This connection indicates that the Atris, in their original conception, were personifications of natural forces, particularly those relating to the atmosphere and the heavens. 值得注意的是，如同《梨俱吠陀》中許多其他神聖存在一樣，阿特里神族與自然現象密切相關。他們與*風暴神馬魯特*（*Maruts*）以及*太陽神阿迪提亞*（*Adityas*）相關聯。這種關連表明，在其原初概念中，阿特里神族是自然力量的人格化，特別是與大氣和天體相關的力量。

我注意到您提供的「英文原文」似乎不是標準英文，而是包含梵文人名和術語的混合文本。這段文字看起來像是梵文人名或吠陀傳統中的系譜記載，而非穆勒著作中的英文段落。 無法確認這是否： 1. 掃描或轉錄中出現的誤讀 2. 梵文原文而非英文翻譯 3. 需要上下文脈絡才能正確識別的片段 **建議提供：** - 完整的英文段落（含前後文脈） - 或確認這段文本的來源與性質 如能澄清原文，我將以您所述的學術規範進行精確翻譯。

The Kasyapas were a family of Rishis, mythologically represented as the sons of Daksha. In the Rig-Veda, Kasyapa is invoked as a father of the gods and demons, and as the creator of all living beings. He is said to have married Aditi, and their children were the Adityas. He also married Diti, and their children were the Daityas. He married Danu, and their children were the Danavas. Again, he married the daughters of Daksha—Kadru, Vinata, and others—and their children were the serpents, birds, and other creatures. Thus Kasyapa represents in the Veda the creative principle, the father and procreator of all things. The name of Kasyapa itself is derived from *kas*, which means "to shine" or "to be visible," and *apa*, which in Sanscrit means "water." His mythology shows him as a solar deity in the first instance, though afterwards he becomes a more abstract principle. The epithet applied to him, *Prajapati* (the lord of creatures), is the same as that which was afterwards applied to Brahma, and shows how the different mythological figures were gradually merged into one abstract conception of the Deity.
41. 迦葉婆 3 迦葉婆、阿伐刹羅、阿西特提。
